[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.

- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. [
  {
    "id": "chatcmpl-tool-a4ccf842eb5fc0dd",
    "type": "function",
    "function": {
      "name": "python",
      "arguments": "{\"code\": \"# Let me understand the maze corridors better by looking at where blocks can be\\n# The blocks are 5x5, so they occupy 5 rows and 5 cols\\n# From the ascii, I can see the black corridors at different rows\\n\\n# Let me identify all possible 5x5 positions within the black corridors\\n# by looking at rows where there are 5+ consecutive B cells\\n\\nascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\n\\n# Find all possible block positions (5x5 areas within black corridors)\\n# A block can be at (r, c) if all cells [r:r+5, c:c+5] are within the black shape\\n# (either B or S currently)\\n\\n# Let me first identify the black corridor regions\\n# From the ascii, the black shape is the maze\\n\\n# For the left corridor, find valid column ranges at each row\\nleft_positions = []\\nright_positions = []\\n\\nfor r in range(9, 59):  # maze rows\\n    row = ascii_lines[r] if r < len(ascii_lines) else \\\"\\\"\\n    # Check if this row and next 4 rows have black corridors\\n    if r + 4 >= 59:\\n        continue\\n    valid = True\\n    for dr in range(5):\\n        rr = r + dr\\n        if rr >= len(ascii_lines):\\n            valid = False\\n            break\\n    if not valid:\\n        continue\\n    \\n    # Find consecutive B/S regions in this row\\n    # Left corridor\\n    left_start = None\\n    left_end = None\\n    for c in range(5, 32):\\n        ch = row[c] if c < len(row) else ''\\n        if ch == 'B':\\n            if left_start is None:\\n                left_start = c\\n            left_end = c + 1\\n        else:\\n            if left_start is not None and left_end - left_start >= 5:\\n                # Check if all 5 rows have B here\\n                can_place = True\\n                for dr in range(5):\\n                    rr = r + dr\\n                    rrow = ascii_lines[rr] if rr < len(ascii_lines) else \\\"\\\"\\n                    for dc in range(5):\\n                        cc = left_start + dc\\n                        ch2 = rrow[cc] if cc < len(rrow) else ''\\n                        if ch2 not in ('B', 'S'):\\n                            can_place = False\\n                            break\\n                    if not can_place:\\n                        break\\n                if can_place:\\n                    left_positions.append((r, left_start))\\n            left_start = None\\n            left_end = None\\n    \\n    # Right corridor  \\n    right_start = None\\n    right_end = None\\n    for c in range(32, 55):\\n        ch = row[c] if c < len(row) else ''\\n        if ch == 'B':\\n            if right_start is None:\\n                right_start = c\\n            right_end = c + 1\\n        else:\\n            if right_start is not None and right_end - right_start >= 5:\\n                can_place = True\\n                for dr in range(5):\\n                    rr = r + dr\\n                    rrow = ascii_lines[rr] if rr < len(ascii_lines) else \\\"\\\"\\n                    for dc in range(5):\\n                        cc = right_start + dc\\n                        ch2 = rrow[cc] if cc < len(rrow) else ''\\n                        if ch2 not in ('B', 'S'):\\n                            can_place = False\\n                            break\\n                    if not can_place:\\n                        break\\n                if can_place:\\n                    right_positions.append((r, right_start))\\n            right_start = None\\n            right_end = None\\n\\nprint(f\\\"Left corridor valid positions: {len(left_positions)}\\\")\\nprint(f\\\"Right corridor valid positions: {len(right_positions)}\\\")\\nprint(f\\\"Left positions: {left_positions[:10]}...\\\")\\nprint(f\\\"Right positions: {right_positions[:10]}...\\\")\"}"
    }
  }
]

[THINKING]
Now I can see the full maze structure. Let me analyze it:
The black shape forms a maze with two corridors (left and right sides). The blue blocks (S) move within the black corridors.
Looking at the structure:
- Rows 9-13: Full horizontal black bar (top of maze)
- Rows 14-18: Left corridor (cols ~13-31), Right corridor (cols ~34-53)
- Rows 19-23: Left corridor (cols ~8-23), Right corridor (cols ~39-53)
- Rows 24-28: Left corridor (cols ~8-18), Right corridor (cols ~44-53)
- Rows 29-38: Left corridor (cols ~13-18), Right corridor (cols ~49-53)
- Rows 39-43: Left corridor (cols ~8-23), Right corridor (cols ~39-53)
- Rows 44-48: Left corridor (cols ~8-28), Right corridor (cols ~34-53)
- Rows 49-53: Left corridor with blue block (cols ~10-14), Right corridor (cols ~34-53)
- Rows 54-58: Left corridor (cols ~8-28), Right corridor with blue block (cols ~44-48)
The maze has a "pinched" section in the middle (rows 29-38) where the corridors are narrowest.
Now, the blue blocks are 5x5. They move 5 cells at a time. The question is: what's the goal?
Looking at the maze, I notice that the left and right corridors are connected at certain rows. The blocks can move up/down within their respective corridors, and left/right to move between different column positions.
Wait, actually looking more carefully, the LEFT/RIGHT keys move the blocks horizontally within the maze. The left block can move left/right within the left corridor, and the right block can move left/right within the right corridor. But they seem to move in opposite directions (LEFT spreads them apart, RIGHT brings them together).
Actually, let me reconsider. Looking at the ascii, the black shape is a single connected object. The blue blocks are inside the black shape. When I press LEFT, the left block moves left and the right block moves right. When I press RIGHT, the left block moves right and the right block moves left.
The goal might be to get both blocks to the same position, or to specific target positions. Let me look for any special markers or patterns.
Actually, I notice something: the black shape has a "pinched" section in the middle (rows 29-38) where the left corridor is at cols 13-18 and the right corridor is at cols 49-53. This is the narrowest part.
Let me think about what the goal could be:
1. Get both blocks to the same row
2. Get both blocks to specific positions
3. Get both blocks to the "pinched" section
4. Swap the blocks' positions
Let me try to get both blocks to the same row and see if that triggers level completion. Currently:
- Left block: rows 49-53, cols 14-18
- Right block: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
Let me try moving the left block down and the right block up to get them to the same row. But wait, UP/DOWN moves both blocks in the same direction. So I can't independently control them vertically.
Hmm, let me reconsider the movement rules:
- UP: both blocks move up (if possible)
- DOWN: both blocks move down (if possible)
- LEFT: left block moves left, right block moves right
- RIGHT: left block moves right, right block moves left
So the blocks always move vertically together, but horizontally in opposite directions. This means the relative vertical position of the two blocks is fixed once they're at different rows... unless one gets blocked while the other doesn't.
Wait, that's the key! The blocks can get blocked at different positions, so their relative vertical positions can change. Let me explore this more.
Actually, looking at the maze structure, the left and right corridors have different heights at different rows. This means the blocks can be at different vertical positions.
Let me think about the goal differently. Maybe the goal is to get both blocks to the "pinched" section (rows 29-38) where the corridors are narrowest. Or maybe the goal is to get them to specific positions that align with some pattern.
Let me try a different approach: look at the initial state image and see if there are any visual clues about the goal. The initial image showed both blocks at the same row (49-53). Maybe the goal is to get them to a different row.
Actually, let me just try to explore the state space more systematically. Since the state space seems small, I can enumerate all reachable states and look for any special conditions.
Let me first reset to a known state and then do a proper BFS.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me understand the maze corridors better by looking at where blocks can be
# The blocks are 5x5, so they occupy 5 rows and 5 cols
# From the ascii, I can see the black corridors at different rows

# Let me identify all possible 5x5 positions within the black corridors
# by looking at rows where there are 5+ consecutive B cells

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Find all possible block positions (5x5 areas within black corridors)
# A block can be at (r, c) if all cells [r:r+5, c:c+5] are within the black shape
# (either B or S currently)

# Let me first identify the black corridor regions
# From the ascii, the black shape is the maze

# For the left corridor, find valid column ranges at each row
left_positions = []
right_positions = []

for r in range(9, 59):  # maze rows
    row = ascii_lines[r] if r < len(ascii_lines) else ""
    # Check if this row and next 4 rows have black corridors
    if r + 4 >= 59:
        continue
    valid = True
    for dr in range(5):
        rr = r + dr
        if rr >= len(ascii_lines):
            valid = False
            break
    if not valid:
        continue
    
    # Find consecutive B/S regions in this row
    # Left corridor
    left_start = None
    left_end = None
    for c in range(5, 32):
        ch = row[c] if c < len(row) else ''
        if ch == 'B':
            if left_start is None:
                left_start = c
            left_end = c + 1
        else:
            if left_start is not None and left_end - left_start >= 5:
                # Check if all 5 rows have B here
                can_place = True
                for dr in range(5):
                    rr = r + dr
                    rrow = ascii_lines[rr] if rr < len(ascii_lines) else ""
                    for dc in range(5):
                        cc = left_start + dc
                        ch2 = rrow[cc] if cc < len(rrow) else ''
                        if ch2 not in ('B', 'S'):
                            can_place = False
                            break
                    if not can_place:
                        break
                if can_place:
                    left_positions.append((r, left_start))
            left_start = None
            left_end = None
    
    # Right corridor  
    right_start = None
    right_end = None
    for c in range(32, 55):
        ch = row[c] if c < len(row) else ''
        if ch == 'B':
            if right_start is None:
                right_start = c
            right_end = c + 1
        else:
            if right_start is not None and right_end - right_start >= 5:
                can_place = True
                for dr in range(5):
                    rr = r + dr
                    rrow = ascii_lines[rr] if rr < len(ascii_lines) else ""
                    for dc in range(5):
                        cc = right_start + dc
                        ch2 = rrow[cc] if cc < len(rrow) else ''
                        if ch2 not in ('B', 'S'):
                            can_place = False
                            break
                    if not can_place:
                        break
                if can_place:
                    right_positions.append((r, right_start))
            right_start = None
            right_end = None

print(f"Left corridor valid positions: {len(left_positions)}")
print(f"Right corridor valid positions: {len(right_positions)}")
print(f"Left positions: {left_positions[:10]}...")
print(f"Right positions: {right_positions[:10]}...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

      "arguments": "{\"code\": \"# Let me look at the initial frame to understand the starting state\\n# and check if there's any difference from current state\\n\\n# First, let me go back to initial state\\n# Current: left at (49, 14), right at (54, 44)\\n# Need to figure out how to get back\\n\\n# Let me first check the history for the initial state\\nif history:\\n    init_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation\\n    for n in init_seg['nodes']:\\n        if n['color'] == 'S':\\n            print(f\\\"Initial S {n['id']}: {n['boundary']}\\\")\\n\\n# Now let me try to explore by going to the top of the maze\\n# Current: left at (49, 14), right at (54, 44)\\n# Let me try going UP multiple times to reach the top\\n\\ndef get_state():\\n    seg = current_frame.segmentation\\n    positions = []\\n    for n in seg['nodes']:\\n        if n['color'] == 'S':\\n            br = n['boundary']\\n            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]\\n            positions.append((r, c))\\n    positions.sort()\\n    return tuple(positions)\\n\\nprint(\\\"Current:\\\", get_state())\\n\\n# Try going UP many times to reach the top\\nfor i in range(10):\\n    result = action(['UP'])\\n    s = get_state()\\n    print(f\\\"UP {i+1}: {s}, changed={result['board_changed']}\\\")\\n    if not result['board_changed']:\\n        print(\\\"  (blocked)\\\")\\n        break\"}"

    for n in init_seg['nodes']:

            print(f"Initial S {n['id']}: {n['boundary']}")

# Now let me try to explore by going to the top of the maze
# Current: left at (49, 14), right at (54, 44)
# Let me try going UP multiple times to reach the top

def get_state():
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            br = n['boundary']
            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]
            positions.append((r, c))
    positions.sort()
    return tuple(positions)

print("Current:", get_state())

# Try going UP many times to reach the top
for i in range(10):
    result = action(['UP'])
    s = get_state()
    print(f"UP {i+1}: {s}, changed={result['board_changed']}")
    if not result['board_changed']:
        print("  (blocked)")
        break

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me do a proper BFS from current state
# Current: left at (14, 14), right at (39, 44)

def get_state():
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            br = n['boundary']
            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]
            positions.append((r, c))
    positions.sort()
    return tuple(positions)

current = get_state()
print("Current:", current)

# BFS - but we need to actually execute actions
# Since the state space might be large, let's limit the search depth
from collections import deque

visited = {current}
queue = deque([(current, [])])
max_depth = 15
goal_path = None

while queue:
    if len(queue) > 1000:
        print("Queue too large, stopping")
        break
    
    state, path = queue.popleft()
    
    if len(path) > max_depth:
        continue
    
    # Navigate to this state
    if path:
        for a in path:
            action([a])
    
    actual = get_state()
    if actual != state:
        # State mismatch - skip this branch
        continue
    
    for a in ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT']:
        action([a])
        new_state = get_state()
        changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
        
        if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
            print(f"GOAL! Path: {path + [a]}")
            goal_path = path + [a]
            break
        
        if changed and new_state not in visited:
            visited.add(new_state)
            if len(path) < max_depth:
                queue.append((new_state, path + [a]))
    
    if goal_path:
        break

print(f"Visited {len(visited)} states")
if goal_path:
    print(f"Goal path: {goal_path}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

--- analysis_step=18 | action=52 | 16:10:53 | tool-agent ---
[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model.       "arguments": "{\"code\": \"# Let me check current state and explore more carefully\\ndef get_state():\\n    seg = current_frame.segmentation\\n    positions = []\\n    for n in seg['nodes']:\\n        if n['color'] == 'S':\\n            br = n['boundary']\\n            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]\\n            positions.append((r, c))\\n    positions.sort()\\n    return tuple(positions)\\n\\nprint(\\\"Current state:\\\", get_state())\\n\\n# Let me explore from here by trying each action and recording results\\nfor a in ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT']:\\n    action([a])\\n    s = get_state()\\n    changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)\\n    print(f\\\"{a}: {s}, changed={changed}\\\")\\n    \\n    # Undo\\n    reverse = {'UP': 'DOWN', 'DOWN': 'UP', 'LEFT': 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT': 'LEFT'}\\n    action([reverse[a]])\\n\\nprint(\\\"Back to:\\\", get_state())\"}"

[THINKING]
The BFS only visited 3 states, which means the state space is very small or the navigation is failing. The issue is that when I navigate to a state and then try actions, the state doesn't match because the blocks move independently and the "undo" doesn't work properly.
Let me reconsider the problem. The BFS approach is failing because:
1. The blocks move independently based on their position in the maze
2. When I try to navigate back to a previous state, the blocks might be at different positions than expected
Let me try a different approach: instead of BFS with navigation, let me just explore from the current state by trying all action sequences and recording the resulting states. I'll use a depth-limited search without trying to "undo" actions.
Actually, the problem is that the BFS is failing because after executing a path to reach a state, the actual state doesn't match the expected state. This suggests that the state transitions are not deterministic or that I'm misunderstanding the movement rules.
Let me re-examine the movement rules by carefully observing what happens when I press each direction from a known state.
Current state: left at (14, 14), right at (39, 44) (approximately, based on the BFS output)
Wait, the BFS started from ((14, 14), (39, 44)) but only visited 3 states. Let me check what the current state actually is after the BFS actions.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check current state and explore more carefully
def get_state():
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            br = n['boundary']
            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]
            positions.append((r, c))
    positions.sort()
    return tuple(positions)

print("Current state:", get_state())

# Let me explore from here by trying each action and recording results
for a in ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT']:
    action([a])
    s = get_state()
    changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
    print(f"{a}: {s}, changed={changed}")
    
    # Undo
    reverse = {'UP': 'DOWN', 'DOWN': 'UP', 'LEFT': 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT': 'LEFT'}
    action([reverse[a]])

print("Back to:", get_state())
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

DOWN: ((24, 9), (49, 49)), changed=False
LEFT: ((19, 9), (44, 49)), changed=True
RIGHT: ((19, 19), (44, 39)), changed=True
Back to: ((19, 14), (44, 44))

- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.

- RIGHT: ((19, 19), (44, 39)) - left moved right, right moved left

Actually, looking more carefully, the LEFT action resulted in the same state as after UP. This might mean that LEFT didn't move the blocks but changed something else (like the timer). Or maybe the blocks were already at the leftmost position and couldn't move further left.
Let me think about this differently. The key observation is:
- UP: both blocks move up (if possible)
- DOWN: both blocks move down (if possible)
- LEFT: left block moves left, right block moves right (if possible)
- RIGHT: left block moves right, right block moves left (if possible)
The blocks move 5 cells at a time (since they're 5x5 blocks).
Now, the "undo" issue: when I press DOWN after UP, the blocks don't return to the original position because they might be blocked. For example, if the left block was at row 24 and moved up to row 19, pressing DOWN should move it back to row 24. But the output shows DOWN resulted in ((24, 9), (49, 49)) with changed=False. Wait, that IS the original state! So DOWN did work, but changed=False?
Hmm, let me re-read:
- Current: ((24, 9), (49, 49))
- UP: ((19, 9), (44, 49)), changed=True
- DOWN: ((24, 9), (49, 49)), changed=False
Wait, DOWN resulted in ((24, 9), (49, 49)) which is the original state, but changed=False? That's inconsistent. Unless changed=False means the board didn't change from the previous frame, but the state is still ((24, 9), (49, 49))... but that would mean DOWN didn't do anything.
Actually, I think I misread. Let me re-examine:
- After UP: state is ((19, 9), (44, 49))
- After DOWN: state is ((24, 9), (49, 49)) with changed=False
If changed=False, the board didn't change. But the state went from ((19, 9), (44, 49)) to ((24, 9), (49, 49)). That's a change! Unless changed=False is a bug or I'm misunderstanding what it means.
Wait, maybe changed=False means the board didn't change from the PREVIOUS frame (which was after UP). But the state is different... This is confusing.
Let me just focus on exploring the state space and finding the goal. The key insight is that the blocks move independently within the maze, and I need to find the right configuration to complete the level.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me do a cleaner BFS exploration
# The issue is that undoing doesn't work reliably
# Instead, let me explore from current state and record all reachable states

def get_state():
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            br = n['boundary']
            r, c = br[0][0], br[0][1]
            positions.append((r, c))
    positions.sort()
    return tuple(positions)

# Current state
current = get_state()
print("Current:", current)

# Let me try all 4 actions and see what happens
states_from_current = {}
for a in ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT']:
    action([a])
    s = get_state()
    states_from_current[a] = s
    print(f"{a}: {s}")

# Now go back by trying reverse actions
# But since undo doesn't work, let me just note the transitions
print("\nTransitions from current:")
for a, s in states_from_current.items():
    print(f"  {current} --{a}--> {s}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  ((19, 14), (44, 44)) --RIGHT--> ((19, 14), (44, 44))

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.
